questions.question:
Newspapers are a constant source of delight and recreation for me. The ________ trouble is that I read ________ many of them.

A. even, quite
B. even, too
C. only, quite
D. only, too
questions.to_find: the pair of words that correctly fills both blanks in the sentence

understand.plain: The sentence expresses a mild complaint: the speaker enjoys newspapers so much that they read an excessive number of them. The first blank modifies 'trouble' (limiting it to a single type), and the second blank modifies 'many' (indicating excess). We need to pick the pair that makes the sentence grammatically and semantically correct.

keywords.explain: Used to restrict or limit; here it narrows 'trouble' to one specific kind ('the only trouble is...')
keywords.example: The only problem is I have too much work.

keywords.explain: Means 'excessively' or 'more than desired'; pairs with adjectives/adverbs to signal excess
Means 'fairly' or 'rather'; it softens rather than signals excess, so it does not fit the meaning of reading an excessive number

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence about reading too many newspapers.

given.aim: Select the word pair that makes the sentence grammatically correct and conveys that the speaker reads an excessive number of newspapers

terms.meaning: Needs a word that restricts or singles out — 'only' fits ('the only trouble')
terms.connects: Rules out 'even' because 'even trouble' is awkward as a noun phrase here

terms.meaning: Needs a word that signals excess — 'too many' is a fixed collocation meaning more than desired
terms.connects: 'Quite many' is non-standard; 'too many' is the natural idiom

terms.meaning: Words that naturally go together in standard English usage
terms.example: 'too many' vs 'quite many' — only 'too many' is idiomatic
terms.connects: Collocation knowledge directly determines which option sounds natural

given.plan: Test each pair in the sentence: check if the first blank restricts 'trouble' meaningfully and if the second blank conveys excess with 'many'. Only 'only, too' satisfies both conditions.

steps.title: Analyse the first blank
steps.apply: The sentence structure is: 'The __ trouble is that...'. Options are 'even' or 'only'. 'Even' here would mean 'the even trouble' which is semantically odd. 'Only' means 'the sole/single trouble', creating the natural reading: 'The only trouble is that I read too many of them.' So the first blank = only.
steps.note: 'Even' can introduce surprise ('even the trouble'), but that requires a different sentence structure.

steps.title: Analyse the second blank
steps.apply: The second blank modifies 'many': 'I read __ many of them.' 'Too many' is a standard collocation meaning 'an excessive number'. 'Quite many' is non-standard in formal English; 'quite a few' would be standard. So the second blank = too.
steps.note: 'Too' always signals excess; 'quite' softens — the sentence needs an expression of excess to explain why it is a 'trouble'.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: Substituting: 'The only trouble is that I read too many of them.' — This is grammatically correct, idiomatic, and semantically coherent. Answer = D. only, too.
steps.note: Both words are needed simultaneously; neither 'even, too' nor 'only, quite' produces a natural sentence.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests English vocabulary and collocation knowledge.

questions.question:
John Thomas, an _____ writer, passed away in 2018.

A. imminent
B. prominent
C. eminent
D. dominant
questions.to_find: the word that best describes a highly respected and distinguished writer

understand.plain: The sentence describes a writer who passed away in 2018 and is being remembered positively. We need an adjective that means 'highly respected and distinguished in a field'. The four options are near-homophones or near-synonyms that are commonly confused, so precise word knowledge is tested.

keywords.explain: Famous and respected within a particular field; distinguished by superior rank, ability, or achievement
keywords.example: An eminent scientist who won the Nobel Prize.

keywords.explain: About to happen very soon; impending — refers to events, not people's status
keywords.example: An imminent storm is approaching the coast.

keywords.explain: Well known and important; visible or noticeable — correct in general contexts but 'eminent' is more specific to professional distinction
keywords.example: A prominent politician often appears in the news.

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence about a distinguished writer who passed away.

given.aim: Choose the adjective that most precisely conveys high professional distinction and respect for a writer

terms.meaning: Highly distinguished, respected for expertise — the standard word for intellectual/artistic eminence
terms.example: An eminent jurist, author, or scientist.
terms.connects: Directly fits the description of a well-regarded writer being mourned

terms.meaning: Well-known, visible — correct but more general; does not carry the connotation of professional excellence
terms.connects: Plausible but 'eminent' is the more precise and conventional choice in the context of a respected writer

terms.meaning: Having power or control over others — describes power relationships, not professional esteem
terms.example: A dominant player in the market.
terms.connects: Incorrect for describing a writer's reputation

given.plan: Eliminate options by meaning: 'imminent' refers to future events (not persons), 'dominant' refers to power, 'prominent' means well-known. 'Eminent' most precisely means distinguished and respected in one's field, making it the best fit.

steps.title: Eliminate imminent
steps.apply: 'Imminent' means 'about to happen soon' and applies to events, not people. 'An imminent writer' is meaningless. Eliminated.
steps.note: 'Imminent' and 'eminent' are common homophones that confuse many test-takers.

steps.title: Eliminate dominant
steps.apply: 'Dominant' means having authority or control. It does not describe a writer's literary reputation. Eliminated.
steps.note: Dominant relates to power, not prestige or expertise.

steps.title: Choose between prominent and eminent
steps.apply: Both 'prominent' and 'eminent' can describe a respected writer. However, 'eminent' specifically implies high distinction in a professional or intellectual field, whereas 'prominent' merely means well-known. For a writer being specifically remembered after death, 'eminent' is the stronger, more precise, and expected word. Answer = C. eminent.
steps.note: In GATE GA questions, 'eminent' is the standard correct choice when describing distinguished academics, writers, or professionals.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests precise knowledge of commonly confused English adjectives.

questions.question:
The minister avoided any mention of the issue of women's reservation in the private sector. He was accused of ______ the issue.

A. collaring
B. skirting
C. tying
D. belting
questions.to_find: the word meaning to deliberately avoid or evade discussing an issue

understand.plain: The sentence says the minister deliberately avoided mentioning a particular topic. We need a word that means 'to avoid or evade (a topic or problem) rather than confronting it directly'. The answer is an idiomatic expression derived from the image of going around the edge (skirt) of something.

keywords.explain: To skirt an issue means to avoid dealing with it directly; to go around it rather than confronting it head-on
keywords.example: The politician kept skirting around the question of tax increases.

keywords.explain: To collar someone means to grab them, catch them, or stop them — it does not mean avoiding a topic
keywords.example: The journalist collared the minister in the corridor for a quick interview.

keywords.explain: To belt means to hit hard or to sing loudly — it has no idiomatic meaning related to avoiding topics
keywords.example: She belted out the national anthem with great enthusiasm.

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence about a minister evading a topic.

given.aim: Identify the verb that idiomatically expresses deliberate avoidance of a topic

terms.meaning: An established English idiom meaning to avoid talking about or dealing with a problem directly
terms.example: Stop skirting the issue and tell me what happened.
terms.connects: Matches exactly: the minister 'avoided any mention' = he was skirting the issue

terms.meaning: Grabbing or catching someone — action toward a person, not an avoidance of a topic
terms.example: Police collared the suspect near the station.
terms.connects: Incorrect because it implies confrontation, not avoidance

terms.meaning: Fastening or binding things together — no idiomatic meaning for avoiding discussion
terms.example: She was tying the parcel with string.
terms.connects: Incorrect; 'tying an issue' is not a standard expression

given.plan: Recognise that 'skirting' is a well-known English idiom meaning 'to avoid confronting an issue'. Eliminate the other options which lack this idiomatic sense.

steps.title: Understand the context
steps.apply: The minister 'avoided any mention' of the issue. The blank must mean the same as deliberately avoiding or evading. This is the definition of 'skirting an issue'.
steps.note: The first sentence gives the meaning; the second restates it with a blank — the correct word must be a synonym for 'avoided'.

steps.title: Test each option
steps.apply: A. collaring = grabbing/catching (not avoidance). B. skirting = going around the edge of, avoiding (CORRECT). C. tying = binding (no relevant idiom). D. belting = hitting hard or singing loudly (no relevant idiom). Only B fits.
steps.note: Idiom recognition is key: 'skirting the issue' is a standard phrase in English.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests English idiom knowledge around the phrase 'skirting the issue'.

questions.question:
A final examination is the ____ of a series of evaluations that a student has to go through.

A. culmination
B. consultation
C. desperation
D. insinuation
questions.to_find: the word meaning the highest or final point in a series of events

understand.plain: The sentence describes a final examination as the last and most significant step in a series of evaluations. We need a noun that means 'the highest point' or 'the final result of a process'. The word 'culmination' perfectly captures the idea of something being the concluding peak of a series.

keywords.explain: The highest or climactic point of something, especially as attained after a long time; the final outcome of a process
keywords.example: Winning the championship was the culmination of years of hard training.

keywords.explain: A meeting or discussion for seeking advice or information — not related to being the end point of a series
keywords.example: She had a consultation with her doctor before the surgery.

keywords.explain: An indirect suggestion, especially of something negative; an implied accusation
keywords.example: His speech was full of insinuations about the rival candidate.

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence describing a final exam as the peak of a series of evaluations.

given.aim: Choose the noun that means the final and highest point of a series of events or evaluations

terms.meaning: The top point or final stage of a progression — precisely matches 'the final examination is the ___ of a series'
terms.example: The concert was the culmination of months of rehearsals.
terms.connects: Directly fits: a final exam is the culminating event of the series of evaluations

terms.meaning: A state of hopelessness or extreme urgency — an emotion, not a stage in a process
terms.example: In desperation, she called for help.
terms.connects: Incorrect; it describes an emotional state, not a sequential endpoint

terms.meaning: A discussion or meeting for advice — an event that precedes decisions, not a final peak
terms.example: He sought legal consultation before signing the contract.
terms.connects: Incorrect; it is a preliminary step, not a culminating one

given.plan: Identify the meaning required (final/highest point of a series), then match to 'culmination'. Eliminate options by meaning: 'consultation' = seeking advice, 'desperation' = emotional state, 'insinuation' = indirect suggestion.

steps.title: Identify the required meaning
steps.apply: The sentence describes the 'final examination' as the endpoint of 'a series of evaluations'. The blank must mean 'the final/peak point of a process or series'. This is precisely the meaning of 'culmination'.
steps.note: Key context clue: 'a series... that a student has to go through' — the final exam caps this series.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: B. consultation = seeking advice (does not imply finality or peak). C. desperation = emotional state of hopelessness (irrelevant to a series of exams). D. insinuation = indirect negative suggestion (irrelevant). Only A. culmination fits.
steps.note: All three wrong options differ significantly in meaning from what the sentence requires.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests precise English vocabulary, specifically the meaning of 'culmination'.

questions.question:
Are there enough seats here? There are ____ people here than I expected.

A. many
B. most
C. least
D. more
questions.to_find: the comparative form of an adjective that correctly expresses a greater number than expected

understand.plain: The speaker is comparing the actual number of people present to an expected number, and finds the actual number to be higher. A comparative adjective is needed ('more' = a greater number than something else). 'Many' is not comparative, 'most' is superlative, 'least' compares in the wrong direction.

keywords.explain: The form of an adjective used to compare two quantities; for 'many/much' the comparative is 'more'
keywords.example: There are more students in this class than in the other.

keywords.explain: Comparative of 'many' (countable) and 'much' (uncountable); means a greater number/amount than a reference point
keywords.example: More people attended the event than last year.

keywords.explain: Superlative form, meaning the greatest number among all; requires a defined set being compared, not just one reference

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blank sentence comparing actual versus expected number of people.

given.aim: Select the correct comparative adjective that indicates a greater number than expected

terms.meaning: Standard construction for comparing quantities: 'more X than Y' where Y is the reference
terms.example: There are more chairs than tables in the room.
terms.connects: Matches the sentence pattern '__ people here than I expected'

terms.meaning: Base (positive) form of the adjective — used without a comparison; does not fit 'than' construction
terms.connects: Incorrect: 'many people... than I expected' is not grammatical

terms.meaning: 'Most' = superlative (greatest among all); 'least' = superlative of 'little' (smallest). Both require a set, not just a reference point
terms.example: She had the most votes of all candidates.
terms.connects: Incorrect: 'most/least people... than I expected' is non-standard

given.plan: The sentence has 'than I expected' which requires a comparative adjective, not a positive or superlative form. 'More' is the comparative of 'many', making it the only grammatically correct choice.

steps.title: Recognise the comparative construction
steps.apply: The sentence contains 'than I expected', which triggers the comparative form. In English: positive=many, comparative=more, superlative=most. The blank must be comparative, so the answer is 'more'.
steps.note: The word 'than' is the grammatical signal that a comparative form is required.

steps.title: Verify direction and eliminate
steps.apply: A. many: positive form, not comparative — eliminates. B. most: superlative — does not fit 'than'. C. least: superlative of little/few, wrong direction (implies fewer, but context says more than expected). D. more: comparative of many — fits 'more people than I expected'. Answer = D.
steps.note: 'Least ... than' would imply fewer than expected, but the question implies surprise at more people being present.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests English grammar, specifically comparative adjective forms.

questions.question:
Column-I has statements made by Shanthala; and, Column-II has responses given by Kanishk.

Column-I (Shanthala):
P. This house is in a mess.
Q. I am not happy with the marks given to me.
R. Politics is a subject I avoid talking about.
S. I don't know what this word means.

Column-II (Kanishk):
1. Alright, I won't bring it up during our conversations.
2. Well, you can easily look it up.
3. No problem, let me clear it up for you.
4. Don't worry, I will take it up with your teacher.

Identify the option that has the correct match between Column-I and Column-II.

A. P->2, Q->1, R->S->4
B. P->3, Q->4, R->1, S->2
C. P->4, Q->1, R->S->3
D. P->1, Q->2, R->4, S->3
questions.to_find: the correct matching of Shanthala's statements to Kanishk's responses based on context

understand.plain: Each statement in Column-I expresses a concern or observation, and each response in Column-II addresses one of those concerns. The phrase 'it up' appears in all four responses, but each response uses a different phrasal verb: 'clear it up', 'take it up', 'bring it up', 'look it up'. We need to match each phrasal verb response to the situation it best addresses.

keywords.explain: Each response uses a phrasal verb (look up, take up, bring up, clear up) that naturally fits only one of the statements in Column-I
keywords.example: 'Look it up' = search for information; matches a statement about not knowing a word's meaning.

keywords.explain: A question type requiring logical alignment between two columns based on conversational and contextual logic
keywords.example: If P says 'the house is a mess', the response should be about fixing/cleaning — 'let me clear it up'.

keywords.explain: A verb combined with a preposition or adverb that has a specific idiomatic meaning different from the base verb
keywords.example: 'Take it up with' means to raise a complaint with someone in authority.

understand.visual_alt: A two-column matching table pairing statements by Shanthala with responses by Kanishk.

given.aim: Match each of Shanthala's four statements to the most contextually appropriate response by Kanishk using phrasal verb meanings

terms.term: P: This house is in a mess
terms.meaning: The house is disorganized or dirty — needs someone to tidy or 'clear it up'
terms.example: After the party the house was a mess; we had to clear it up.
terms.connects: Response 3 ('let me clear it up for you') matches — 'clear up' means to tidy

terms.term: Q: Not happy with marks given
terms.meaning: A complaint about grades — needs someone to raise the issue with the teacher ('take it up')
terms.example: If you are unhappy with your grade, take it up with the professor.
terms.connects: Response 4 ('I will take it up with your teacher') matches — 'take up' means to raise a complaint formally

terms.term: R: Politics is a subject I avoid talking about
terms.meaning: Kanishk should promise not to 'bring it up' in conversation — 'bring up' means to introduce a topic
terms.example: Please don't bring up politics at dinner.
terms.connects: Response 1 ('I won't bring it up') matches perfectly

terms.term: S: I don't know what this word means
terms.meaning: Not knowing a word's meaning — the natural advice is to 'look it up' in a dictionary
terms.example: If you don't know the meaning, look it up online.
terms.connects: Response 2 ('you can easily look it up') matches — 'look up' means to search for information

given.plan: Match each statement to the response whose phrasal verb best addresses the concern expressed: P=clear up (tidy), Q=take up (raise complaint), R=bring up (introduce topic), S=look up (find information). This gives P->3, Q->4, R->1, S->2 = Option B.

steps.title: Match P: house in a mess -> Response 3
steps.apply: P says the house is messy. Response 3 says 'let me clear it up for you'. 'Clear up' means to tidy or clean — a direct and logical response to a messy house. P -> 3.
steps.note: 'Clear it up' here refers to tidying the house, not explaining something.

steps.title: Match Q: unhappy with marks -> Response 4
steps.apply: Q expresses dissatisfaction with marks. Response 4 says 'I will take it up with your teacher'. 'Take it up with' means to formally raise a complaint with someone in authority. Q -> 4.
steps.note: 'Take up' implies escalating an issue to someone who can resolve it — here, the teacher.

steps.title: Match R: avoids politics -> Response 1
steps.apply: R says she avoids talking about politics. Response 1 says 'I won't bring it up during our conversations'. 'Bring up' means to introduce a topic in conversation. Kanishk promises not to raise the topic. R -> 1.
steps.note: 'Bring up' = introduce a topic; the response mirrors Shanthala's avoidance request.

steps.title: Match S: doesn't know word meaning -> Response 2
steps.apply: S says she does not know what a word means. Response 2 says 'you can easily look it up'. 'Look up' means to search for information (e.g., in a dictionary). S -> 2.
steps.note: 'Look up' is the standard idiom for finding a word's meaning in a reference source.

steps.title: Identify matching option
steps.apply: The mapping is P->3, Q->4, R->1, S->2. Comparing with options: Option B = P->3, Q->4, R->1, S->2. Answer = B.
steps.note: All four phrasal verbs (clear up, take up, bring up, look up) have distinct meanings that uniquely determine the matching.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests contextual understanding of English phrasal verbs and conversational logic.
